step_by_step_thinking:
Review of the medical records reveals that the patient complains of numbness in his hands and feet, generalized weakness, and constipation, indicating a possibility of a neuropsychiatric issue related to trace elements. The radiographic finding of linear lines on the metaphyses of knee and wrist joints suggests metastatic calcification associated with certain diseases like Faber disease. However, there was mention of heavy metals being involved in similar presentations. Therefore:
\boxed{A} Based on the key symptomatology described - hand-foot-mouth manifestations indicative of nervous system involvement alongside unusual radiological findings pointing towards deposition issues possibly linked to heavy-metal toxicity, checking serum mercury levels would be prudent first step before ruling out other possibilities through subsequent targeted diagnostics. Thus, option A directly addresses the likely causative agent implicated here.
(Note: It appears I mistakenly referred to 'hand-foot-mouth' instead of 'hands-feet.' Correcting now.) Based on the key symptomatology described – numbness in hands and feet, generalized weakness, and constipation, suggestive of a neural-metabolic issue potentially involving trace elements or heavy metals, together with the radiographic finding of linear lines on the metaphyses of knee and wrist joints hinting at deposition-related pathology consistent with heavy metal intoxication. Checking serum mercury levels thus represents the appropriate initial investigation.
